title: Lumbee Fairness Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
plain_summary: The Lumbee Fairness Act gives full federal recognition to the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina by changing a 1956 law. This means the Lumbee people can now get all the same services and benefits that other federally recognized tribes receive. The government can also hold land in trust for the tribe, which helps protect their territory. North Carolina will continue to handle legal matters on tribal land unless the state and tribe agree to change this arrangement.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Lumbee Fairness Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Federal Recognition
sections.text: This section changes a law from nineteen fifty six about the Lumbee people. The changes do several things. First, it removes a section that prevented the Lumbee people from getting federal recognition as an Indian tribe. Second, it reorganizes the old law by turning the introduction into official findings by Congress. These findings explain that Congress believes certain facts about the Lumbee people are true. Third, it adds new definitions to make the law clearer. The Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior, who runs the government department that handles Indian affairs. The Tribe means the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina or the Lumbee Indians of North Carolina. Fourth, it adds new sections that give the Lumbee people full federal recognition as an Indian tribe. The law says the federal government now recognizes the Lumbee Tribe, which was known as petitioner number sixty five by the Office of Federal Acknowledgment. Once recognized, all federal laws that apply to Indian tribes and their members will also apply to the Lumbee Tribe and its members. The law also says that other groups of Indians in Robeson County and nearby counties in North Carolina can still apply for recognition if their members are not already enrolled in the Lumbee Tribe. The law makes the Lumbee Tribe and its members eligible for all services and benefits that the federal government provides to recognized Indian tribes. For the purpose of getting these services, Lumbee people living in Robeson, Cumberland, Hoke, and Scotland counties in North Carolina will be treated as if they live on or near an Indian reservation. The Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Health and Human Services must work with the Lumbee Tribe to figure out what services the tribe needs. They must then tell Congress in writing what those needs are. The law says the tribal membership roll that exists when this law passes will be used to determine who gets federal services, after the Secretary of the Interior checks that it is correct. The Secretary can only check that people on the roll meet the membership rules in the tribe's constitution from November sixteen, two thousand one. This checking must be finished within two years after the tribe gives the Secretary a digital copy of the roll with supporting documents. The law allows the Secretary of the Interior to take land and hold it in trust for the benefit of the Lumbee Tribe. When the tribe asks for land in Robeson County, North Carolina to be put in trust, the Secretary must treat it the same way as land that would be put in trust on an existing reservation. The law says that North Carolina state government will continue to have legal authority over land owned by or held in trust for the Lumbee Tribe. This means North Carolina courts will handle criminal cases and civil lawsuits that happen on Lumbee land. However, the Secretary of the Interior can accept a transfer of some of this authority from North Carolina to the federal government if North Carolina and the Lumbee Tribe make an agreement about it. Any such transfer cannot happen until two years after the agreement is made. The law makes clear that none of these changes affect how the Indian Child Welfare Act works.
